unit.name: Maximum likelihood estimation and autoregressive factorization
unit.description: Develops exact-density training via maximum likelihood and shows how autoregressive factorization turns sequence modeling into next-token prediction.
unit.summary: To move from taxonomy to concrete models, the lecture introduces maximum likelihood estimation as the generic way to fit an explicit density model: write a neural network for p(x; W), then choose parameters that make the observed training samples most likely under the model [ts=2114s] [ts=2153s]. Assuming IID data lets the joint likelihood factor across samples, and taking logs converts products into sums, producing the familiar log-likelihood objective used to train neural networks [ts=2265s] [ts=2296s] [ts=2321s]. Autoregressive models add the key structural assumption that each sample x can be split into an ordered sequence of subparts x1...xT, after which the chain rule gives a product of conditional next-part distributions [ts=2348s] [ts=2386s] [ts=2416s]. This makes RNNs and masked transformers natural autoregressive architectures for language [ts=2444s] [ts=2480s], but applying the same idea to images by rasterizing subpixel values into a huge discrete sequence is computationally expensive, which explains both the historical difficulty of pixel-level autoregressive image models and the need for more efficient tokenizations [ts=2582s] [ts=2653s] [ts=2693s].

key_points.text: Maximum likelihood estimation chooses model parameters that maximize the likelihood of the observed training data.
Assuming IID data lets the joint dataset likelihood factor into a product over independent samples, and log-likelihood turns that product into a sum.
Autoregressive models factor p(x) using the chain rule over an ordered sequence of subparts x1, x2, ..., xT.
RNNs and masked transformers implement autoregressive next-step prediction naturally.
Text fits autoregressive modeling well because it is already a 1D discrete sequence.
Rasterizing images into long sequences of subpixel values makes autoregressive image modeling feasible in principle but very expensive.
